speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Huppert, tell me what you-- continue to please tell me what you found in Sosnowiec when you went home after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came the long journey, I came to my hometown, to Czeladz, to Sosnowiec. But we lived, like I mentioned, in Czeladz. And I came. That was on Sunday. And there was emptiness-- no friends, no relatives, no cousins, no kin, no one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did see they're still hanging the old-- the lock from our store, the same lock. And I went in the back of our house where we had the garden. And a young girl looked through the window. And she ask me, who are you looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was just motionless. I couldn't move or speak then. I just left and never returned again. And one of the tenants recognized me-- was surprised that they see me still alive-- someone Jew to be alive. And one tenant, she said, thank God. There's somebody to take over back the house. And she told me that she saw my mom marching and-- to work, and they took her-- how she-- bad she look, how thin she was. [SIGHS] And my mom used to help her even, I think with her-- she helped us in the beginning. She gave me some milk for my-- for me and my sister. Mom probably gave her plenty too for it. And then she said mommy even helped her daughter too for the funeral. But the rest of the tenants, they looked at me if-- with some surprise that I'm still here. And then I went to the people that I was in hiding, the one who was-- I mean with the-- with-- I walked in. The daughter, the people who already knew the customers, she wasn't there. But the old woman was there, the grandmother. And I thought how rude and cruel her heart-- how mean. I couldn't believe my-- I walked in, and I saw the bedspread, what belonged to us. Obviously, mom probably gave as a gift. And she didn't even offer a little water. Then I went to this director, which she helped my brother to go to this-- to the college, to the engineering college. She spoke about eight languages. And with this caliber of a person, and being so intelligent and no children-- she lost her husband herself. They'd been all directors and very famous people, and being so nice to us. I came up to her-- I-- that was my second stop. The rest, I didn't want to go and see no one. She didn't answer, didn't offer how are you, offer a coffee, or tea, milk, glass of something, water-- not a sound. And I was very heartbroken-- disappointed. And I said, that's the end of it. I was-- say I will never return again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to sell the house. I didn't want to do anything. I said my-- what do I need this for? So my-- if my brother lives, let him take care of it. I didn't pay attention anymore to nothing. Where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia. But he got out the same way, because of the diploma as a Pole, as a Polish citizen. There was General Sikorski, like I was told-- my brother told me. When he heard that Polish citizens, they were gonna alight-- relieve from Siberia, he obviously-- what he had still the diploma, he showed to him as a citizen. He-- they got out Poland-- from Siberia. That's how he came to look for anyone, some relatives. And that was in the Jewish community, because I been. So they said they would write the names where there was survived. He didn't believe that I was alive, that I survived. I was too delicate. Nobody believed anything that I-- never-- not my cousin-- nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- something very important point I forgot. While our-- my daddy was under krankenstube after they transferred-- can I go back to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When from Markstadt, when they liquidated, they sent all the [NON-ENGLISH]. Some of them went to Reichenbach, which they'd been lucky. And my daddy, with whoever rest of them remain in Markstadt, they sent to Funfteichen, which was the worst camp. It was a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Funfteichen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funfteichen, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. And of the fellow-- one neighbor, a son, which I didn't know he was also in Markstadt-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He, after, tried to look for me. Because why? He was, when daddy on death bed, he was dying-- passing-- dying-- he came to see him in the krankenstube. I don't know how he got there. But Daddy told him, look. I don't know if anyone is alive, if my-- if [NON-ENGLISH] is alive, I don't know. The rest of the family, I don't know for any of my-- of my wife. But he knew that I was there. He said, she was, you know, the apple of my eye. And if you-- after the war, if you find her, if you look for her, remember to help. You know how it was at home. And give me your hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the death bed, he has to give him his hand. That's how they-- he had to swear that he will look for me after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] Which I found him in Munich, and he asked me if I can be of any help. I couldn't accept anything. We always been used to help others. That was it. I said no, I didn't need. That time already I met my husband. Anyway I was already engaged. Because I think if you know how I met my husband-- yeah, I'm coming back home to Peterswaldau.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Polish girl. She didn't know I was Jewish. In the street car, she asked her father-- was a-- on the railroad had-- how you call? [NON-ENGLISH]. He was at the head of the-- Engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: --engineering, whatever-- in charge. And she was only daughter, only child. I didn't know. She told me, she says, since you had no transportation-- and I also didn't tell her. I told her Helen Zarasinska instead of [NON-ENGLISH] Zarasinska, because [NON-ENGLISH] was too obviously being Jewish. And I was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, why don't you stay with us until the connection? You know, next day is supposed to be the connection to some-- to go to Peterswaldau. And she introduced me to her father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she asked the father if she-- and allow me to stay with them. And the father, sure. If Hela-- if-- if [NON-ENGLISH] likes you-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- if [NON-ENGLISH] likes you, I will have two daughters. And if they tried to talk me out of it, I shouldn't go back to Peterswaldau. If you buy me anything-- but I tried to excuse myself. I need my things from home. Then I will come back, I promise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime he says, why do you need things? I can buy anything you want. Here what I buy for [NON-ENGLISH] buy for you. If she is happy-- you know, I want her to make happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they lived in German-- with a German flat, a German woman-- beautiful apartment. So the old-- the older woman, she-- I went-- while I was waiting for the-- the connection-- was talking to her. And I spoke German too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, just too bad, she said to my husband. I don't have my husband. I would love to have you, adopt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what the irony of my life. I say if they knew what-- who I am-- [LAUGHS] that I am Jewish, she had tried to destroy us. And they want to adopt you-- how the irony of life can be. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I left-- excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanna get back on-- on the story. So you were in Sosnowiec after the war. You went back to Peterswaldau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go back there. There's no other way. I didn't want to stay in Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so then where did you go from Peterswaldau?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Peterswaldau I went-- I was with this cousins. I guess it was the two ladies. One I didn't know, then was a cousin of my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had already met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not then.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also with the other lady-- but then I went back again to Sosnowiec, because she ask me-- the cousin-- to go back to home to help the cousin, whatever. I couldn't refuse the brother or sister. And she-- she-- they found each other, the sister-- the younger sister. And then while I was walking the street-- that was [NON-ENGLISH] in Sosnowiec-- with her-- she noticed a young man walking, and this was my husband. She said, you know, if you excuse me, I think I-- this young man must be a relative. And so I said, OK. And so she approached him, and she asked him and said, I think they come. The name was [NON-ENGLISH]. Now one of the [NON-ENGLISH], they were very well to do-- the cousins I knew. I-- I didn't really know, but my cousin knew. But there was a resort in [NON-ENGLISH] They used to come there, and they had a villa. So all the [NON-ENGLISH], they knew. They had a gold mine-- coal mine in [NON-ENGLISH]. They call [NON-ENGLISH], in Dumbrava. That was their-- what they owned. It was a cousin. And he looked so much like him, because they looked all tall and handsome. They'd been-- in the family, they been very good looking young men. Now, I didn't-- he wasn't my friend, because he was much older, but my cousin did. And they-- I remember they went back when they talked while we were evacuating. They would always talk to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, this is my cousin. And then so she walked to him-- toward him. And they got, naturally, very excited. And so he came back to Peterswaldau with her, to the sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was-- we would start to talk, and I wanted to go to the doctor, to the Dr. Freed. But I was afraid to cross the border, because I didn't ever been there by myself, or walking the border, and that's something to me a monster. I couldn't visualize to be able to do it by myself, and I was afraid, especially right after the war. There were soldiers and all. There's no transportation. It was very-- something impossible for me to think. But then he-- we stayed together for-- I need-- he said, I would help you to go. My brother know all the-- the roads. And we can help you to get to.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we get there-- and we got back to Sosnowiec. And he was with all his cousins and with the-- and whole friends-- a clique. And he said, why do you wanna go to Hungary? And then we start to talk. They talk me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we start-- we-- I remain in Sosnowiec. And then we got engaged. We didn't think we were marrying then, because who was thinking of anything to marry. It was too early. But anyway, we got engaged later on. And that's how-- my husband was just after Dachau. He had not a penny. [LAUGHS] He was very himself in a bad situation. But-- and that's how I remained with him and didn't go to Hungary. And that's why we went together from there to Czechoslovakia. Because that's-- we have to-- we wanted to go to the American side rather than with the Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go from-- from when you passed through Czechoslovakia, where-- where did you go with--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Prague we went together with the cousins. They all went together. We went to-- I think it was Schwandorf-- the first stop. And then it was [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we went to-- the [NON-ENGLISH] and knew where they got all the people they want-- wanted to emigrate during that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ended up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went all Schwandorf. That was Germany. Schwandorf was Germany. And we went to Deggendorf. I think it was the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so, and your husband's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moris Huppert.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband-- [LAUGHS] right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the interviews what-- are separate. So-- so you and Moris went to Deggendorf. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --is that where you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his-- yes. And his brother and the cousins. And we went on together in Deggendorf. And that's where--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we got married May the 1st, 1946. And after that, after six weeks, my brother came from-- from Poland. My husband was very ill at that time. He got an infection. German made him infection in the tooth-- lucky he was alive. Anyway, a Jewish doctor, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] was very nice and helpful. And so he saw then his-- when he woke up after the surgery, he said, I think I saw your brother come in. Meantime, his brother, the younger brother-- Joe, who's now in Chicago-- came running. Your brother came. I couldn't believe. [CRYING] And then my sister-in-law came together. We supposed to go all emigrate to Israel, because that was the only thing we wanted to be. That was no Israel then. That was just Palestine. There wasn't turmoil then between the English and, you know, the-- and there were this Cyprus. And it called the exodus then. And my doctor, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], he told me you would never survive. I wouldn't let you go. We were very close with the wife and the family-- and very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: They been to the wedding. And in fact, we've been contact here. He was in New York, and that's how we-- where was I? Well, let-- let me go back a little bit. What was it-- so what was it like being a young newlywed just after the war in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- it was horrible. I never visualized something like this, to live [LAUGHS] like this. But we-- it was crowded. We had no place to stay. We're all together. It was a terrible feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came, my sister-in-law with the baby. It was born in Siberia. And he said, I don't know how we-- because how can you live like this? So I say, look, that was just a temporary thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to emigrate, but it's the only solution we could have being there in DP camp in order to emigrate. Otherwise, you had no choice. And we have to remain in Germany, which we didn't want to stay. And there was no other way out from the situation. And my brother and my sister-in-law, she had a sister before the war, who she lived in Paris. And she never knew her. She was 10 years older, 15 years older. It was a big difference in age. But she knew that she was alive. And I think she wanted to-- she wasn't sure if she survived. But obviously, maybe they did find out. I don't remember. But she says she wants to go to Paris to stay with-- with her sister. And then my husband helped them out-- first my brother, and then him. It was a difficult time. It was a very difficult-- there was no-- no honeymoon. [LAUGHS] There were no-- we had no really. It was really very sad. But I think that for us, as long as we were been alive, I think that-- that's the only thing what matter. We were hoping for better future, to get out from this-- from Germany and to hope for-- for our-- to make the best of our life. This would give us strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a child in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I, yeah, I got-- and expected-- I expected with my daughter, Rita. And I went difficult time. I was a child myself, like my doctor said. This is how it happened-- a child has a child. But it went very wonderful to me. And I wind up after-- I think when-- she almost-- I almost died while she were birthed-- the child into the world. She was a breech baby. In the German hospital, the-- the doctor who was in charge was an SS and killed a lot of Jewish babies, and killed his own [NON-ENGLISH] that she's own-- this woman who helped to bring the children to world-- to the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it effect was when he came out, told my husband that he-- or I be alive-- or what did he want? He had two choices-- or I should live or my baby-- the baby. So Moris says, I want them both alive. If not, you're gonna be dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole camp, wherever they live in Germany, in the camp, Deggendorf-- one lady, my neighbor, which I got a letter, she was a very dear person to me. I needed a momma. [LAUGHS] So she was about 36. She was beautiful people. I still have it, and she wrote it. I think she lived in-- in Montreal. I got a beautiful letter from her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she passed out. Everybody else was crying. And now they still live here a man who remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the camp, everybody was on bicycles in front of the hospital. And they say if there [LAUGHS] gonna something happen, they were gonna kill. And there was-- they still remember this. And he, sure enough, he got scared. And then two doctors, I recall, and they-- he said the last minute, I think he put some anesthetic. And he screamed at me he wanted a live child. [GERMAN]. That's what he shouted at me. He wanted a live child. And I couldn't take it. I just passed out. And sure enough, she-- and they woke me-- say, you see, the beautiful [NON-ENGLISH]. This means the beautiful baby. And that was my daughter, and everybody loved her. She was everybody's baby. My-- my-- a friend of mine who she's in Israel, she said that the kids were jealous of her, because all that she should talk was about Rita and even having her own. And she was so-- was a-- they been wonderful people. When you felt so close with people who loved you so much, that made a little bit easier, the life, to struggle-- the hard times, this hardship.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Deggendorf? And where did you go with Rita and your husband, Moris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we went, we went first to-- and we-- we registered to United States. But then we had some difficulty in Bremerhaven, I think, and then we went to Canada. In Canada, something happened what my husband didn't like, and then we went to United States. And we came to United States. We helped-- my husband helped a lot of people to get through-- the consul and doctor, whatever. He was very helpful, always been. He sacrificed all his life always for others, being in camp or without, even up till the last minute, even today. It-- even after the war, we got-- we constantly-- whenever we-- every year, practically, we went to Israel. There was practically nothing in Israel-- was no hotels, no airport. We had nothing, just the beginning. That was right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a difficult time for-- for them as well as for us, and we tried to share whatever we had. We send them extra thing always with the suitcases. My brother used to say, what do you carry in this? And I say, there was all full of clothes, and the bedspread, and sheets, which are quite heavy, and money, whatever we could. Because I could-- I got letters. I still have. I cannot hear the cry of the children. How could I eat if I know this kind of letter? I get something like this from people that I didn't even know, but I think as a human being you cannot just ignore pain like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were our people. That was-- even if I didn't know them, I think they need us, and-- and we-- whatever we could, we share. We bought the first bonds-- what we had, whatever we could. We helped-- medication, anything-- medicine. And they remember, some of that-- them. We would come to Israel. There, everybody comes, no matter from where. And I found people what they-- accidentally, I visit my husband's friends, which from their hometown. Somebody came to visit them. And she said, you look so familiar. And she heard the name, Sosnowiec. I told her my maiden name. She asked me, so I told her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her father was my daddy's best friend. That's funny how close-- how funny how things are-- circumstance can bring together people. And we constantly correspond with each other-- and very devoted-- and a lot of people which we are close with. Tell me about the life that you and Moris built here, about the work that you did, and about raising your daughter, and-- and any grandchildren that you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one-- first of all, I have only one daughter, which her name is Rita. My married name is Frumin. And she finished college.

speaker: interviewer
text: She got a-- a bachelor degree-- architecture, but she doesn't use her profession. She very talented. And-- can you kindly repeat to come and refresh my memory? Well, tell me-- tell me first--

speaker: interviewee
text: How we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell me about what you and you-- you and Moris did. What-- what kind of life did you make for yourself here in terms of what work did you do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I work-- first of all, I did apply to associate, although-- but difficult to speak English. But I was desperate. I need something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I applied to Kelly Company, that they take off the-- from the wages. And I got the job going through the intelligent test, which I passed. And I think he-- the manager thought I'm German, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, what happen, I applied to the office, and I was working for the associate investment company before we opened our store, our own business. And until-- they-- they didn't know I was Jewish. But when it came to Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, I was a good worker. I tried to do everything my best, because I need the money, even whatever they pay was not this like today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the manager just walked in, and I ask the manager, Mr. Giles, can I have the two days-- I never forget the name. Can I have the two days? It's New Year's Eve, and I--

speaker: interviewee
text: That time came out my English perfect. I don't know how I got it. It was on so short time in being United States. It wasn't too easy to cope with language. Anyway, there was sitting next to him a American girl. Naturally, most of them all been degree, with a law degree. My destiny was at home. My parents anyway, they always wanted me to be in law, a lawyer. Which the war interrupt everything, and our dream was shattered.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, so he-- she was next to the manager. She interrupted us, whatever she was doing. And he looked at me. And I never forget this scene. He said, you're not Jewish. He pulled out the drawer from the desk with such a fury, and anger, and redness in his face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at him. I was shocked. He said, you're not Jewish, and pulled back, you know, pushed back the drawer to the desk. You're not Jewish, Rose.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked at him. Is there anything wrong, Mr. Giles? Yes, I am Jewish. So he said-- he looked at me, and he says, no, you're not Jewish! I say, yes I am. So he say, you came from Germany. You're German.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I came from Germany, but I'm not Jewish. I'm not German. I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, I don't know. We just have one lawyer within-- with-- which in the legal department-- you know, because they're different departments. So he says, I have to call the home office in South Bend and see would they allow me or not to take off the two days-- take only one day, Rosh Hashanah, the Yom Kippur, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, Yom Kippur-- the New Year's Eve, Rosh Hashanah, New Year's Eve and Yom Kippur, the day of the atonement. I say, and if you believe in God, and your respect your holiday, I want you to respect mine. And even if I don't have-- I don't want pay, but I won't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he walked out to him. He say, I'll let you know. I say, good. I went back to my file, whatever I was doing then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back. And he was-- he put me on-- his hand on mine shoulder. And he says, OK, Rose. You've got your days off. And then was at-- from that time on, he used to brag so much about me, brag that I'm a good worker, and all this-- just yackety-yack. There was one Jewish girl. She was a college degree. She worked at the telephone. And when I told her about it, she said, oh, Rose. I said, there's so many antisemitism. She said, oh, Rose. You're so sensitive, because you came from the-- from Europe, you went so much through. Sure enough, Yom-- Yom Kippur came around, Rosh Hashanah. She did not ask for permission, because she felt so sure it's a holiday which she had-- can take off. When she came back, they fired her. Then she came and she say, you know, you're so right. Now, he was very-- a-- he was completely different person. He never went down with his head, never faced me, never say hello. But then when I-- I quit, and because we want to open our own business, which I don't know if my husband mentioned to you to as how we started. We went to the-- we went without money. [LAUGHS] We had the courage-- there's a chutzpah, like they say in Jewish. We went to the money. We know that they had a beautiful hotel in downtown, the special-- all those manufacturers came, Oleg Cassini, Pierre Cardin, [NON-ENGLISH], all those-- Oscar de la Renta, all the finest manufacturers which we deal-- dealt with, and Mr. Singer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we came and told him. So he say, what can I do for you young people? So we tell him-- we told him the situation. We'd like to open a business, but we have no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he say, you know what? If you trust-- I said, I send you merchandise. You-- I give you-- today I don't think you could have done this, because everything goes through the banks, and that you didn't get to meet the manufacturer. At that time, you had them personal basis. It was different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they were wonderful to us. And they gave us, send us merchandise. And more I wanted, the more I would-- probably I would get as much as I-- we wanted. And I was-- then I became secretary in our own business and also homemaker, which I am proud of. I think that's also the most responsible profession in life for every lady, for every woman, to bring up the proper way a-- a family and responsibility, to make them be mensch, to be people the way we would like them to be. We tried our best, anyway. Now, going back to when I quit, I still got the letter from Mr. Paul Shore. I never forget. He was the vice president of the associate from South-- South Bend. I received a letter, if anyone said anything to me, and if I'd like to change to another department, they would be glad to do. So they come. And I was a good worker, obviously, because all the auditors when they came, they said since we have Rose, everything is perfect, and all the things. But I could sense there was a lot in our surrounding, there were a few one that were lovely people. Matter of fact, I still receive-- but she was working in the legal department, not where I was-- I still get Christmas and Hanukkah card. I still receive now-- Ethel Krinberg, such a wonderful lady. That's, you know, makes you feel that the world is not lost. There are still good people in it. And I wish there would be more of them like her, because wonderful people. And we still correspond with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we move into any kind of closing remarks, tell me about your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren-- I think my have the greatest grandchildren. Everybody love my daughter, naturally, but I'm going now to the second generation, which I think I'm proud of them. And I'm grateful to God that he blessed us at least with some comfort to have those two boys. Which they're very devoted, very good, not only for us, but I think they are so sincere. There are not too many young people you find them today, they respect their pay for grandparents, for parents, for anyone. And they are very intelligent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name is Zaron-- the oldest is Zaron. He just graduate from Michigan with seven honors. He was the peer student selected from the senate of the students-- peer student of the year. He had so many-- he had so many degrees and so many awards. He's so great. And my grandson, Jonathan, which is also great, intelligent. He got also many reward. And they want both to be, I think one is taking law and business degree. They want to prepare themselves for the course-- I mean, what they-- they have to go through essays-- whatever they call this special test they going through. And this-- the younger one's in Tucson, which is the third, I think, year of college. And they-- they would like to be together, so.
